description: Specifies a name for a query block, facilitating precise control of the scope of other hints through this name; the original default name of the query block remains valid.

Instructs the optimizer to use the Sort Merge Join algorithm for the specified tables, suitable for scenarios with large data volumes or insufficient memory; it uses less memory but may have longer execution time.
Instructs the optimizer not to use the Sort Merge Join algorithm for the specified tables, avoiding its use in unsuitable scenarios.

Instructs the optimizer to use the Index Nested Loop Join algorithm for the specified tables, suitable for scenarios where the result set of the outer table after filtering is small (fewer than 10,000 rows).
Instructs the optimizer not to use the Index Nested Loop Join algorithm for the specified tables, avoiding its use in unsuitable scenarios.

Instructs the optimizer to use the Index Nested Loop Hash Join algorithm, which builds a hash table on the outer table with a fixed upper limit on memory usage; applicable scenarios are consistent with INL_JOIN.
Instructs the optimizer not to use the Index Nested Loop Hash Join algorithm for the specified tables, avoiding its use in unsuitable scenarios.
Instructs the optimizer to use the Index Nested Loop Merge Join algorithm; applicable scenarios are consistent with INL_JOIN.
Instructs the optimizer not to use the Index Nested Loop Merge Join algorithm for the specified tables, avoiding its use in unsuitable scenarios.
Instructs the optimizer to use the Hash Join algorithm for the specified tables, which executes concurrently with multiple threads, is fast but consumes more memory.
Instructs the optimizer not to use the Hash Join algorithm for the specified tables, avoiding its use in scenarios such as insufficient memory.
Instructs the optimizer to use the Hash Join algorithm for the specified tables and use them as the Build side to construct the hash table.
Instructs the optimizer to use the Hash Join algorithm for the specified tables and use them as the Probe side to execute the hash join.
Instructs the optimizer to rewrite semi-joins (such as EXISTS subqueries) into ordinary inner joins, expanding the range of execution methods the optimizer can choose to improve efficiency.
Takes effect only in MPP mode; instructs the optimizer to use the Shuffle Join algorithm for the specified tables, and can be combined with HASH_JOIN_BUILD/Probe to control the Build/Probe sides.
Takes effect only in MPP mode; instructs the optimizer to use the Broadcast Join algorithm for the specified tables, and can be combined with HASH_JOIN_BUILD/Probe to control the Build/Probe sides.
Instructs the optimizer not to decorrelate correlated subqueries in the specified query block, using the Apply operator for execution, suitable for scenarios where decorrelation reduces efficiency.

Instructs the optimizer to use the Hash Aggregation algorithm for all aggregate functions in the specified query block, which executes concurrently with multiple threads, is fast but consumes more memory.

Instructs the optimizer to use the Stream Aggregation algorithm for all aggregate functions in the specified query block, which uses less memory but may have longer execution time, suitable for large data volumes or insufficient memory.
Takes effect only in MPP mode; instructs the optimizer to use the one-phase aggregation algorithm for all aggregate functions in the specified query block.
Takes effect only in MPP mode; instructs the optimizer to use the two-phase aggregation algorithm for all aggregate functions in the specified query block.
Instructs the optimizer to use only the given indexes for the specified table; if no indexes are specified, it means no indexes are used (full table scan).
Has the same effect as USE_INDEX; instructs the optimizer to use only the given indexes for the specified table.
Instructs the optimizer to ignore the given indexes for the specified table, not considering their use.
Instructs the optimizer to use only the given indexes for the specified table and read them in order, suitable for scenarios requiring ordered index reading (testing is needed to avoid errors).
Instructs the optimizer to use only the given indexes for the specified table but not read them in order, suitable for scenarios where ordered index reading is unnecessary.
Instructs the optimizer to push down aggregate functions in the specified query block to the coprocessor, improving aggregation efficiency.
Instructs the optimizer to push down Limit and TopN operators in the specified query block to the coprocessor, improving query efficiency.
Instructs the optimizer to read the specified tables from the specified storage engines (TIKV or TIFLASH); the table alias (if any) must be used.
Instructs the optimizer to access the specified table through index merging (union or intersection type); can specify an index list or use all available indexes.
Instructs the optimizer to generate a multi-table join plan in the order of table names in the hint, more versatile than STRAIGHT_JOIN.

Used in non-recursive common table expression queries to disable subquery materialization and inline internal queries into external queries, improving efficiency in specific scenarios (such as pushing down external conditions).
Disables the optimizer's index merge function, with higher priority than USE_INDEX_MERGE; must be placed in the outermost query to take effect for the entire query.
Controls whether to convert in (subquery) to join and aggregation; TRUE enables it, FALSE disables it.
Limits the statement execution time to N milliseconds; the execution is terminated if it times out, with higher priority than system variables.
Limits the memory usage of statement execution (supports MB/GB units); when exceeded, it behaves according to settings (logging or termination).
Enables reading data from consistent TiKV follower nodes to improve read performance.
Forces the current prepare statement not to use the plan cache when Prepared Plan Cache is enabled.
Temporarily modifies the session-level system variable value, which is restored after the statement execution; used to adjust optimizer/executor behavior.
Instructs the optimizer to generate a join plan in the order of table names in the FROM clause, with higher priority than LEADING.
Instructs the optimizer to select the Nth physical plan found during the physical optimization phase (mainly for testing, compatibility not guaranteed).
Temporarily uses the specified resource group to execute the statement for resource control; attention to permission control is required.
